revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Ako Shrine (Takabatake-cho, Nara)]][[ja:赤穂神社]]
<!--generated by generate_shikinaisha_pages_v25_with_redirects.py-->
{{Expand Japanese|赤穂神社}}

{{Infobox religious building
| name = Akabo Shrine
| native_name = 赤穂神社
| image = Akoh Shrine1.jpg
| mapframe = yes
| mapframe-zoom = 15
| mapframe-wikidata = yes
| mapframe-point = none
| map_type = Japan
| map_caption = Akabo Shrine
| coordinates = {{coord|34.676902|135.84265}}
| map_relief = 1
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|fr|Ama-no-Koyane-no-mikoto|it|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|天児屋命|mni|ꯑꯃꯦ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯣꯌꯅꯦ|pt|Ame-no-Koyane|ru|Амэ-но коянэ-но микото|simple|Ame-no-Koyane|zh|天兒屋命|qid=Q2564508|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}
| website = {{Official website|http://www1.kcn.ne.jp/~fujishrs/profile_akabo.html}}
}}

{{nihongo|'''Akabo Shrine'''|赤穂神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

== instance of (P31) ==

* {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}}
* {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|qid=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}}
* {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|lt=Shikinai Shōsha|en|Shikinai Shosha|ja|式内小社|simple|Shikinai Shosha|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}
* {{ill|Kanpei-sha|lt=Kanpei-sha|simple|Kanpei-sha (Engishiki Jinmyocho)|qid=Q135160338|1=Kanpei-sha}}<ref>Kokugakuin University Shrine database</ref>
* {{ill|Shikinai Hiteisha|lt=Shikinai Hiteisha|ja|比定社|qid=Q135026601|1=Shikinai Hiteisha}}

== part of (P361) ==

* {{ill|Q11433427|ltq=Q11433427|qid=Q11433427|1=List of Shikinaisha in Yamato Province}}
** Series ordinal: 19
** P155: {{ill|Q714559|ltq=Q714559|qid=Q714559|1=Kasuga-taisha}}
** P156: {{ill|Shimada Shrine|lt=Shimada Shrine|ja|嶋田神社|qid=Q24876134|1=Shimada Shrine}}

== Japanese content ==
{{Otheruses|A shrine in Nara City|A shrine enshrining the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Forty-seven rōnin|1=UNKNOWN}}|Ōishi Shrine}}
{{Shinto shrine
|Name=Akabo Shrine
|Image=[[Image:Akoh Shrine1.jpg|240px|]]
|Location={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nara City|1=UNKNOWN}} Takabatake-chō 1320
|Deity={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane|qid=Q2564508}}
|Rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai shōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}, former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Village shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}{{sfn|History of Nara Prefecture: Shrines|p=21}}
|Founded=
|Main hall=
|Annual festival=
|Rituals=
|ISO = JP-29
|Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 40|Latitude seconds = 36.8
|Longitude degrees =135|Longitude minutes = 50|Longitude seconds = 33.6
|Map   = Nara city
|Label position = bottom
}}
'''Akabo Shrine''' (あかぼじんじゃ<ref>[http://www1.kcn.ne.jp/~fujishrs/profile_akabo.html Nanto Kagami Shrine Official Website]</ref>) is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinto shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takabatake-chō (Nara City)|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nara City|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}.

It is a shrine listed in the '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki|1=UNKNOWN}}', and in the '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Register of Deities|1=UNKNOWN}}' read aloud during the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Omizutori|1=UNKNOWN}} ceremony at {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nigatsu-dō|1=UNKNOWN}}, it appears as “Akaho Myōjin.”

The '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=UNKNOWN}}' records that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Princess Tōchi|1=UNKNOWN}} was buried in the land of “Akaho” on April 14 of the 6th year of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=678|1=UNKNOWN}}), and that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hikami no Musume|1=UNKNOWN}} was buried there in the 10th year of Emperor Tenmu ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=682|1=UNKNOWN}}). This site is therefore considered a strong candidate for the location referred to as Akaho.

=== Enshrined deities ===
The principal enshrined deity is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane|qid=Q2564508}}, and shrines dedicated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tenmangū|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten|1=Benzaiten}} are also enshrined together on the grounds.

There is also an oral tradition that it “enshrines a noble princess,” and it is said to have divine virtues for the protection of women.

=== History ===
It is said to have been the tutelary shrine of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fujiwara clan|1=UNKNOWN}} since the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Asuka period|1=UNKNOWN}}, and to have been relocated to this site in connection with the transfer of the capital to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Heijō-kyō|1=UNKNOWN}}<ref name="yuisho">{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nanto Kagami Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} pamphlet</ref>. It is said that ripe rice ears were hung on a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=himorogi|1=UNKNOWN}}, and the words “Please partake of the red ear of rice” were spoken, from which it came to be called the Shrine of Akaho<ref name="yuisho" />.

It is said that originally the shrine grounds covered several hundred tsubo, with a large torii gate on the street of Myōi-chō (perhaps {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Akai-an|1=UNKNOWN}}), and that it also possessed shrine lands famous for cherry blossoms<ref name="yuisho" />. In later years the land became rice fields, and the area came to be called “Sakurata” (Cherry Blossom Fields)<ref name="yuisho" />.

With the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=UNKNOWN}}, the shrine fell into ruin; it is said that nearly 200 households and most of the shrine priests dispersed, leaving only the earthen wall standing desolately, and that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tatsuo Hori|1=UNKNOWN}} and others left poems and prose lamenting its state. Moved by this, local volunteers worked to restore it, enshrining Tenmangū and Benzaiten together and placing them to the left of Akabo Shrine, resulting in the present form of two shrines standing side by side.

In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1930|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shōwa|1=UNKNOWN}} 5), it became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nanto Kagami Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, located near {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinyakushi-ji|1=UNKNOWN}} in the neighborhood, and remains so today<ref name="yuisho" />.

=== Legends ===
When {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=UNKNOWN}} suffered from an eye disease, he prayed at {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinyakushi-ji|1=UNKNOWN}}, and in a dream received a divine oracle saying, “Pray to the god of Akaho and wash your eyes with the pure water to the southwest of the shrine”<ref name="yuisho" />. When he did so, he was immediately cured. Because of this, the spring was named Myōi, and is now called {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Akai-an|1=UNKNOWN}}<ref name="yuisho" />.

=== Access ===
From the Nara Kōtsū city loop or inner loop bus, get off at “Warawareishi-chō” and walk east for 5 minutes.
